design_philosophy.core_loop: Descend through labyrinth levels solving puzzles, acquiring items, managing resources (gold, spells, potions), engaging in sword combat and magic casting against diverse enemies
design_philosophy.primary_tension: High lethality with opaque mechanics—many items and actions can instantly kill the player without clear warning; cryptic puzzle solutions and trap-based deaths create constant danger
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Elderly wizard rescuing mentor from deep labyrinth while uncovering an ancient conspiracy; progression through dangerous dungeon with magical tools and allies
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Brutally difficult arcade-style action disguised as RPG; refuses to hold player hand on puzzle solutions or trap mechanics; high graphic violence for 1990 Nintendo release; linear path masked by exploration; dreams and NPC dialogue deliver dark narrative of dragon genocide and betrayal

difficulty.continues_system: Password system allowing mid-game resume; corrupted passwords trigger debug screens
difficulty.checkpointing: Password-based progression saving; straw beds in labyrinth serve as rest/narrative delivery points
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Minimal—no difficulty selection documented; puzzle solutions are cryptic and failure often means instant death; item usage can be lethal if wrong choice made

progression.upgrade_system: Sword combat with dodge/swing/stab; magical items (fireball spells, levitation, magic carpet, floating barrel); potions and equipment purchased or looted
progression.unlock_mechanism: Items acquired from merchant or found in chests/bodies required to progress (spores, gold, bait mentioned as required for later levels); goblins become allies after king is spared; Ulindor provides assistance and key items
replayability_hooks: Cryptic puzzle solutions encourage replaying to discover mechanics
Multiple enemy types and hazards create varied encounters
Item acquisition order affects gameplay viability

world_building_techniques: Dream visions on straw beds reveal ancient dragon civilization of Erinoch and its destruction
NPC dialogue (Ulindor, merchant, Dunric) gradually reveals conspiracy
Environmental encounters (goblin-troll war, merchant assistance) establish world politics
Twist revelation: Mordamir is both mentor figure and merchant disguise; kidnapper orchestrating trap
Backstory of dragon genocide and revenge cycle drives final boss encounter

boss_design.philosophy: Final confrontation synthesizes narrative twist—Mordamir (ancient survivor and last dragon) represents culmination of 1000-year revenge cycle against player's civilization
notable_bosses: Mordamir (final boss)—ancient wizard and last surviving dragon; only boss explicitly named in sources

faction_indicators: Goblins vs. Trolls war; player can ally with goblins by sparing their king
Mordamir represents hidden antagonist faction (ancient dragon survivors)
Merchant (Mordamir in disguise) as false ally figure
danger_signals: Invisible shades, flying lizards, will-o'-the-wisps indicate supernatural threats
Flesh-eating slimes, man-eating worms, colossal spider establish escalating biological horror
Water norfalc as unique late-game threat
Instant-death items and deathtraps signal environmental lethality
Ulindor potentially killable by goblin at level start (unused mechanic) suggests ally vulnerability
environmental_storytelling: Labyrinth structure itself represents dragon dungeon formerly controlled by creatures
Straw beds as rest points where dreams reveal history—meditation as lore delivery
Merchant stalls and goblin/troll encounters establish living dungeon ecosystem
Treasure and corpse looting suggests prior adventurer deaths
Ancient city of Erinoch vision conveys lost civilization above dungeon
